---
title: "Replication: Machine Learning-Based Fake News Detection"
subtitle: "Implementation of Zeng & Tao (2024)"
author: "Akul Srivatsa and Steven Ky"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# Global options for the document
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

```{r}
library(tm)           # Text mining
library(SnowballC)    # Word stemming
library(tidyverse)    # Data manipulation & ggplot2
library(caret)        # ML workflow and CV
library(e1071)        # Naive Bayes implementation
library(randomForest) # Random Forest implementation
library(nnet)         # Native R MLP implementation
library(dplyr)        # Data transformation
```

```{r}
# Load dataset
df <- read.csv("buzzfeed_combined.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# Feature Engineering: Multimedia metadata
# Converting link lists into binary indicators (0 = absent, 1 = present)
df$has_images <- ifelse(is.na(df$images) | df$images == "" | df$images == "[]", 0, 1)
df$has_movies <- ifelse(is.na(df$movies) | df$movies == "" | df$movies == "[]", 0, 1)
```

```{r}
media_sources <- unique(df$source)

# Create and clean the text corpus
corpus <- VCorpus(VectorSource(df$text)) %>%
  tm_map(content_transformer(tolower)) %>%
  tm_map(removeNumbers) %>%
  tm_map(removePunctuation) %>%
  tm_map(removeWords, stopwords("english")) %>%
  tm_map(removeWords, tolower(media_sources)) %>%
  tm_map(stemDocument) %>%
  tm_map(stripWhitespace)

# Create Document-Term Matrix (DTM)
dtm_mat <- as.matrix(DocumentTermMatrix(corpus))
labels <- as.factor(make.names(df$label))
```

```{r}
# Calculate p-values for word significance
p_vals <- apply(dtm_mat, 2, function(x) {
  if(length(unique(x > 0)) < 2) return(1)
  chisq.test(table(x > 0, labels))$p.value
})

# Filter for significant terms (p < 0.05)
sig_terms <- names(p_vals[p_vals < 0.05])
final_data <- as.data.frame(dtm_mat[, sig_terms])

# Integrate non-textual features and sanitize column names
final_data$has_images <- df$has_images
final_data$has_movies <- df$has_movies
final_data$label <- labels
names(final_data) <- make.names(names(final_data))

# Remove Near-Zero Variance predictors
nzv <- nearZeroVar(final_data)
if(length(nzv) > 0) final_data <- final_data[, -nzv]
```

```{r}

set.seed(123)
nb_folds <- createFolds(final_data$label, k = 5)

nb_results <- sapply(nb_folds, function(indices) {
  model <- naiveBayes(label ~ ., data = final_data[-indices, ], laplace = 1)
  preds <- predict(model, final_data[indices, ])
  confusionMatrix(preds, final_data[indices, ]$label)$overall["Accuracy"]
})

cat("Naive Bayes Mean CV Accuracy:", mean(nb_results), "\n")
```

```{r}
set.seed(123)
lr_model <- train(label ~ ., data = final_data, method = "glmnet",
                  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5),
                  family = "binomial")
print(max(lr_model$results$Accuracy))
```

```{r}
set.seed(123)
# 500 trees used per parent paper methodology
rf_model <- randomForest(label ~ ., data = final_data, ntree = 500)
print(rf_model)
```

```{r}
# Data Sanitization and Scaling
mlp_input <- final_data %>%
  dplyr::mutate(label = as.factor(label)) %>%
  dplyr::filter(complete.cases(.))

# Min-Max Normalization for neural network stability
x_cols <- setdiff(names(mlp_input), "label")
mlp_input[x_cols] <- lapply(mlp_input[x_cols], function(x) {
  if(max(x) - min(x) == 0) return(x) 
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
})

# Training with nnet to avoid TensorFlow backend dependencies
set.seed(123)
mlp_fit <- caret::train(
  label ~ ., 
  data = mlp_input, 
  method = "nnet", 
  trControl = caret::trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5),
  tuneGrid = expand.grid(size = c(10, 15), decay = c(0.1, 0.5)),
  maxit = 500,
  MaxNWts = 10000,
  trace = FALSE
)

cat("MLP Replicated Accuracy:", round(max(mlp_fit$results$Accuracy), 4), "\n")
```

```{r}
# TABLE I: Performance Comparison Table (Dynamic Version)
# 1. Extract Accuracy from your specific model outputs
# Naive Bayes: Mean of CV results
nb_acc <- mean(nb_results)

# Random Forest: (Correct Predictions / Total Predictions) from confusion matrix
rf_acc <- sum(diag(rf_model$confusion[, 1:2])) / sum(rf_model$confusion[, 1:2])

# Logistic Regression: Max accuracy from the glmnet tuning grid
lr_acc <- max(lr_model$results$Accuracy)

# MLP: Max accuracy from the nnet tuning grid
mlp_acc <- max(mlp_fit$results$Accuracy, na.rm = TRUE)

# 2. Build the Comparison Data Frame
# Benchmarks are from Zeng & Tao (2024)
performance_table <- data.frame(
  Classifiers = c("Naive Bayes", "Random Forest", "Logistic Regression", "Multi Layer Perceptron"),
  Replicated_Accuracy = round(c(nb_acc, rf_acc, lr_acc, mlp_acc), 4),
  Parent_Paper_Benchmark = c(0.654, 0.836, 0.764, 0.909)
)

# 3. Format as a professional table
knitr::kable(performance_table, 
             caption = "TABLE I: PERFORMANCE COMPARISON OF VARIOUS CLASSIFIERS",
             col.names = c("Classifiers", "Replicated Accuracy", "Zeng & Tao (2024) Benchmark"))
```

```{r}
# Fig. 1: Statistical bar chart of real and fake news sources
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Prepare source count data
source_counts <- df %>%
  group_by(source, label) %>%
  tally() %>%
  mutate(label = ifelse(label == 0, "Real", "Fake"))

# Plot for Fake News Sources (matches top half of Fig 1)
ggplot(filter(source_counts, label == "Fake"), aes(x = reorder(source, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#2980b9") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Source count of fake news in Buzzfeed", x = "source", y = "count")

# Plot for Real News Sources (matches bottom half of Fig 1)
ggplot(filter(source_counts, label == "Real"), aes(x = reorder(source, n), y = n)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", fill = "#c0392b") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Source count of real news in Buzzfeed", x = "source", y = "count")

# Fig. 2: Comparison chart of common sources
# Filters for sources that appeared in both categories
common_sources <- source_counts %>%
  group_by(source) %>%
  filter(n() > 1)

ggplot(common_sources, aes(x = source, y = n, fill = label)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "stack") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Fake" = "#2980b9", "Real" = "#c0392b")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Common source of real and fake news in Buzzfeed", x = "Source", y = "Count")
```

```{r}
# Function to get top words by label frequency
get_top_words <- function(dtm, labels, top_n = 20) {
  word_freqs <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(dtm))
  word_freqs$label <- labels
  
  # Melt and aggregate
  melted <- word_freqs %>%
    pivot_longer(cols = -label, names_to = "word", values_to = "count") %>%
    group_by(word, label) %>%
    summarise(total = sum(count)) %>%
    mutate(label = ifelse(label == "X0", "Real", "Fake"))
  
  # Identify top discriminatory words
  top_words <- melted %>%
    group_by(word) %>%
    summarise(grand_total = sum(total)) %>%
    arrange(desc(grand_total)) %>%
    head(top_n) %>%
    pull(word)
    
  return(filter(melted, word %in% top_words))
}

# Fig. 3: High Frequency Words in Titles (Top 20)
# Note: Requires a separate DTM created from just titles
title_data <- get_top_words(dtm_mat, labels, 20) # Replace dtm_mat with title DTM if available

ggplot(title_data, aes(x = reorder(word, total), y = total, fill = label)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Fake" = "#2980b9", "Real" = "#c0392b")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Most discriminatory words in the title of news", x = "Top 20 words", y = "Term Frequency of words")

# Fig. 4: High Frequency Words in Bodies (Top 30)
body_data <- get_top_words(dtm_mat, labels, 30)

ggplot(body_data, aes(x = reorder(word, total), y = total, fill = label)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Fake" = "#2980b9", "Real" = "#c0392b")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Most discriminatory words in the body of news article", x = "Top 30 words", y = "Term Frequency of words")
```
```{r}
# 6. Novel Implementation: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)
# Load necessary libraries for explainability
library(fastshap)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# 1. Prepare the Prediction Wrapper
# SHAP requires a custom function that returns a single numeric probability
p_fun <- function(object, newdata) {
  predict(object, newdata = newdata, type = "prob")[, "X1"]
}

# 2. Select an Instance to Explain
# We'll explain the model's decision for the first article in the dataset
set.seed(123)
baseline <- mlp_input[sample(1:nrow(mlp_input), 10), x_cols] # Background distribution
instance <- mlp_input[1, x_cols, drop = FALSE]              # The specific article

# 3. Calculate SHAP Values
# This calculates the "contribution" of each feature to the final probability
shap_values <- fastshap::explain(
  mlp_fit,
  X = as.matrix(mlp_input[, x_cols]),
  pred_wrapper = p_fun,
  nsim = 50, # Number of Monte Carlo simulations
  newdata = as.matrix(instance)
)

# 4. Prepare for Visualization
shap_df <- data.frame(
  Feature = names(instance),
  ShapValue = as.numeric(shap_values)
) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(ShapValue))) %>%
  slice(1:15) # Show top 15 contributing words

# 5. Visualizing the "Evidence"
ggplot(shap_df, aes(x = reorder(Feature, ShapValue), y = ShapValue, fill = ShapValue > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#e74c3c", "FALSE" = "#2ecc71"), 
                    labels = c("Pushes toward Real", "Pushes toward Fake")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "SHAP Explanation: Why was this article flagged?",
    subtitle = "Positive values (Red) increase the 'Fake News' probability score",
    x = "Linguistic Feature",
    y = "SHAP Value (Contribution)",
    fill = "Impact"
  )
```

```{r}
# 7. Novel Implementation: Adversarial Stress Test
# 1. Identify a 'Fake' article that the model classified correctly
# We use the instance from our SHAP analysis
original_probs <- predict(mlp_fit, newdata = instance, type = "prob")
cat("Original Prediction (Fake News Probability):", round(original_probs$X1, 4), "\n")

# 2. Create an 'Adversarial' Version
# We manually 'perturb' the data by removing the top 3 red-flag features 
# identified by SHAP: 'sound', 'buy', and 'wouldn.t'
adversarial_instance <- instance
adversarial_instance$sound <- 0
adversarial_instance$buy <- 0
adversarial_instance$wouldn.t <- 0

# 3. Test the Model's Resilience
adversarial_probs <- predict(mlp_fit, newdata = adversarial_instance, type = "prob")
cat("Adversarial Prediction (After removing red flags):", round(adversarial_probs$X1, 4), "\n")

# 4. Impact Analysis
drop_in_confidence <- original_probs$X1 - adversarial_probs$X1
cat("Drop in Model Confidence:", round(drop_in_confidence * 100, 2), "%\n")

# 5. Visualization of Robustness
robustness_df <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Original Article", "Adversarial Article"),
  Confidence = c(original_probs$X1, adversarial_probs$X1)
)

ggplot(robustness_df, aes(x = Scenario, y = Confidence, fill = Scenario)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Model Robustness: Adversarial Sensitivity Test",
       subtitle = "Does removing 'Red Flag' words change the classification?",
       y = "Probability of being 'Fake'") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Original Article" = "#e74c3c", "Adversarial Article" = "#f39c12"))
```

```{r}
# 8. Novel Implementation: Synonym Substitution Test (with Logging)
# 1. Identify the 'Fake' article instance and its original probability
original_prob <- predict(mlp_fit, newdata = instance, type = "prob")$X1
cat("--- ADVERSARIAL AUDIT START ---\n")
cat("Original Prediction (Fake News Probability):", round(original_prob, 4), "\n\n")

# 2. Create the 'Synonym-Swapped' Instance
synonym_instance <- instance

# Function to safely swap values and print status
safe_swap <- function(data_inst, target_word, synonym_word) {
  if (target_word %in% names(data_inst) && data_inst[[target_word]] > 0) {
    if (synonym_word %in% names(data_inst)) {
      # Perform the swap: Transfer weight to synonym
      data_inst[[synonym_word]] <- data_inst[[synonym_word]] + data_inst[[target_word]]
      data_inst[[target_word]] <- 0
      cat(paste0("SUCCESS: '", target_word, "' swapped for '", synonym_word, "'\n"))
    } else {
      # Fallback: Word is removed because synonym wasn't a significant feature
      data_inst[[target_word]] <- 0
      cat(paste0("NOTICE: '", target_word, "' removed (Synonym '", synonym_word, "' not in feature set)\n"))
    }
  }
  return(data_inst)
}

# 3. Execute Swaps based on SHAP Red Flags
synonym_instance <- safe_swap(synonym_instance, "sound", "audio")
synonym_instance <- safe_swap(synonym_instance, "buy", "purchas")
synonym_instance <- safe_swap(synonym_instance, "wouldn.t", "not")

# 4. Test the Model's Semantic Robustness
synonym_prob <- predict(mlp_fit, newdata = synonym_instance, type = "prob")$X1
cat("\nAdversarial Prediction (After Swaps):", round(synonym_prob, 4), "\n")
cat("--- ADVERSARIAL AUDIT END ---\n")

# 5. Result Visualization
comparison_df <- data.frame(
  Method = c("Original Article", "Synonym Swapped"),
  Confidence = c(original_prob, synonym_prob)
)

ggplot(comparison_df, aes(x = Method, y = Confidence, fill = Method)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.4) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Semantic Robustness: Synonym Substitution Test",
       subtitle = "Checking if the model tracks 'Meaning' or just 'Specific Words'",
       y = "Probability of 'Fake'") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Original Article" = "#e74c3c", "Synonym Swapped" = "#3498db"))
```
